content.problemStatement:
A formal statement is provided after the story, you may skip to it.

Everule has a very weird way of buying handbags when he visits the luxury street.

There are NNN luxury stores in the luxury street, and they are numbered 1,2,‚Ä¶,N1, 2, \ldots, N1,2,‚Ä¶,N. The cost of the handbag from the stores can be represented by an array CCC of size NNN, i.e, the iii-th shop sells handbags at a cost of CiC_iCi‚Äã.

Everule buys the handbag from the iii-th shop at the cost of CiC_iCi‚Äã if and only if CiC_iCi‚Äã is not lesser than the average of his previously bought handbags. (He always buys from the first shop he visits, which may not be shop numbered 111).

A normal human might have tried to go through the shops in order; but Everule is sporadic, so Dominater knows he visits each shop exactly once, but he doesn't know the order in which Everule visits them.

Dominater is very interested to know how much Everule spent, but since that depends on the order in which Everule visited the shops, he asks you to tell him whether it is possible for Everule to have spent exactly KKK instead, for each KKK in [1,‚àëi=1NCi][1, \sum_{i = 1}^{N} C_i][1,‚àëi=1N‚ÄãCi‚Äã].

Formally, given the array CCC of size NNN, and a permutation PPP of {1,2,‚Ä¶,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,‚Ä¶,N}, the process can be defined as follows:


Initialize sum=CP1\text{sum} = C_{P_1}sum=CP1‚Äã‚Äã, cnt=1\text{cnt} = 1cnt=1.
For each 2‚â§i‚â§N2 \le i \le N2‚â§i‚â§N, if and only if CPi‚â•sumcntC_{P_i} \ge \frac{\text{sum}}{\text{cnt}}CPi‚Äã‚Äã‚â•cntsum‚Äã, update sum‚Üêsum+CPi,cnt‚Üêcnt+1\text{sum} \gets \text{sum} + C_{P_i}, \text{cnt} \gets \text{cnt} + 1sum‚Üêsum+CPi‚Äã‚Äã,cnt‚Üêcnt+1.

(Do not round the fraction sumcnt\frac{\text{sum}}{\text{cnt}}cntsum‚Äã).
The score of the permutation PPP is defined to be the value of sum\text{sum}sum at the end of the process.

For each KKK such that 1‚â§K‚â§‚àëi=1NCi1 \le K \le \sum_{i = 1}^{N} C_i1‚â§K‚â§‚àëi=1N‚ÄãCi‚Äã, output 111 if there exists any permutation PPP such that the score of PPP is KKK, and 000 otherwise.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN, the number of stores.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers, C1,C2,‚Ä¶,CNC_1, C_2, \ldots, C_NC1‚Äã,C2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,CN‚Äã ‚Äî the costs of handbags at each store.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line a binary string SSS of length ‚àëi=1NCi\sum_{i = 1}^{N} C_i‚àëi=1N‚ÄãCi‚Äã, where SkS_kSk‚Äã is 111 if and only if there exists at least one permutation PPP with score = KKK.
constraints: The sum of all Ci over all test cases does not exceed 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the number of stores. The second line contains N space‚Äëseparated integers C1, C2, ‚Ä¶, CN ‚Äì the costs of the handbags.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a binary string S of length equal to the sum of all Ci in that test case. For each K from 1 to Œ£Ci, the K‚Äëth character of S is '1' if there exists a permutation of the shops whose final spent amount equals K, otherwise it is '0'. Print each string on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The scores of all possible permutations are as follows:
P=[1,2,3]P = [1, 2, 3]P=[1,2,3] : initially sum=C1=1,cnt=1\text{sum} = C_1 = 1, \text{cnt} = 1sum=C1‚Äã=1,cnt=1.

i=2i = 2i=2, Pi=2P_i = 2Pi‚Äã=2 ‚Äî since C2‚â•11,C_2 \ge \frac {1}{1}, C2‚Äã‚â•11‚Äã, updated sum=sum+C2=3,cnt=cnt+1=2 \text{sum} = \text{sum} + C_2 = 3, \text{cnt} = \text{cnt}  + 1 = 2sum=sum+C2‚Äã=3,cnt=cnt+1=2.
i=3i = 3i=3, Pi=3P_i = 3Pi‚Äã=3 ‚Äî since C3‚â•32,C_3 \ge \frac {3}{2}, C3‚Äã‚â•23‚Äã, updated sum=sum+C3=6,cnt=cnt+1=3 \text{sum} = \text{sum} + C_3 = 6, \text{cnt} = \text{cnt} + 1 = 3sum=sum+C3‚Äã=6,cnt=cnt+1=3.

Thus, the score of this permutation is 666.
P=[1,3,2]P = [1, 3, 2]P=[1,3,2] : initially sum=C1=1,cnt=1\text{sum} = C_1 = 1, \text{cnt} = 1sum=C1‚Äã=1,cnt=1.

i=2i = 2i=2, Pi=3P_i = 3Pi‚Äã=3 ‚Äî since C3‚â•11,C_3 \ge \frac {1}{1}, C3‚Äã‚â•11‚Äã, updated sum=sum+C3=4,cnt=cnt+1=2 \text{sum} = \text{sum} + C_3 = 4, \text{cnt} = \text{cnt}  + 1 = 2sum=sum+C3‚Äã=4,cnt=cnt+1=2.
i=3i = 3i=3, Pi=2P_i = 2Pi‚Äã=2 ‚Äî since C2‚â•42,C_2 \ge \frac {4}{2}, C2‚Äã‚â•24‚Äã, updated sum=sum+C2=6,cnt=cnt+1=3 \text{sum} = \text{sum} + C_2 = 6, \text{cnt} = \text{cnt} + 1 = 3sum=sum+C2‚Äã=6,cnt=cnt+1=3.

Thus, the score of this permutation is 666.
P=[2,1,3]P = [2, 1, 3]P=[2,1,3] : initially sum=C2=2,cnt=1\text{sum} = C_2 = 2, \text{cnt} = 1sum=C2‚Äã=2,cnt=1.

i=2i = 2i=2, Pi=1P_i = 1Pi‚Äã=1 ‚Äî since C1<21C_1 < \frac {2}{1}C1‚Äã<12‚Äã, we do no updates.
i=3i = 3i=3, Pi=3P_i = 3Pi‚Äã=3 ‚Äî since C3‚â•21,C_3 \ge \frac {2}{1}, C3‚Äã‚â•12‚Äã, updated sum=sum+C3=5,cnt=cnt+1=2 \text{sum} = \text{sum} + C_3 = 5, \text{cnt} = \text{cnt} + 1 = 2sum=sum+C3‚Äã=5,cnt=cnt+1=2.

Thus, the score of this permutation is 555.
P=[2,3,1]P = [2, 3, 1]P=[2,3,1] : initially sum=C2=2,cnt=1\text{sum} = C_2 = 2, \text{cnt} = 1sum=C2‚Äã=2,cnt=1.

i=2i = 2i=2, Pi=3P_i = 3Pi‚Äã=3 ‚Äî since C3‚â•21,C_3 \ge \frac {2}{1}, C3‚Äã‚â•12‚Äã, updated sum=sum+C3=5,cnt=cnt+1=2 \text{sum} = \text{sum} + C_3 = 5, \text{cnt} = \text{cnt} + 1 = 2sum=sum+C3‚Äã=5,cnt=cnt+1=2.
i=3i = 3i=3, Pi=1P_i = 1Pi‚Äã=1 ‚Äî since C1<52C_1 < \frac {5}{2}C1‚Äã<25‚Äã, we do no updates.

Thus, the score of this permutation is 555.
P=[3,1,2]P = [3, 1, 2]P=[3,1,2] : initially sum=C3=3,cnt=1\text{sum} = C_3 = 3, \text{cnt} = 1sum=C3‚Äã=3,cnt=1.

i=2i = 2i=2, Pi=1P_i = 1Pi‚Äã=1 ‚Äî since C1<31C_1 < \frac {3}{1}C1‚Äã<13‚Äã, we do no updates.
i=3i = 3i=3, Pi=2P_i = 2Pi‚Äã=2 ‚Äî since C2<31C_2 < \frac {3}{1}C2‚Äã<13‚Äã, we do no updates.

Thus, the score of this permutation is 333.
P=[3,2,1]P = [3, 2, 1]P=[3,2,1] : initially sum=C3=3,cnt=1\text{sum} = C_3 = 3, \text{cnt} = 1sum=C3‚Äã=3,cnt=1.

i=2i = 2i=2, Pi=2P_i = 2Pi‚Äã=2 ‚Äî since C2<31C_2 < \frac {3}{1}C2‚Äã<13‚Äã, we do no updates.
i=3i = 3i=3, Pi=1P_i = 1Pi‚Äã=1 ‚Äî since C1<31C_1 < \frac {3}{1}C1‚Äã<13‚Äã, we do no updates.

Thus, the score of this permutation is 333.
It can be clearly seen that we can only obtain permutations with score = 3,53, 53,5 or 666 in the first test case.
In the second test case, it can be shown that regardless of our initial permutation PPP, we always end up having to update sum\text{sum}sum for each element, thus each permutation PPP has score = 333.

solution.approach: Sort the costs in non‚Äëincreasing order. Let pref[i] be the sum of the first i elements (the i largest costs) and let nxt be the (i+1)-th element (or 0 if i = N). A subset of shops can be the first i elements of this order iff pref[i] > i * nxt. This condition is equivalent to the requirement that every shop not taken has a cost strictly smaller than the average of the taken shops, which guarantees that a permutation exists whose final sum equals pref[i]. Therefore the only attainable final sums are those pref[i] that satisfy the inequality. For each test case we compute all such prefix sums, mark them in a boolean array of size Œ£Ci, and finally output a binary string where positions corresponding to marked sums are '1'. The algorithm runs in O(N log N) per test case for sorting, plus O(Œ£Ci) to build the output string, which fits the constraints.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N + Œ£Ci) per test case (sorting dominates). The total over all test cases is O( (total N) log (max N) + total Œ£Ci ), well within limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + Œ£Ci) per test case for the sorted array, prefix sums and the reachable‚Äësum boolean array.
